_note: ORIGINAL content. Topic coverage follows David Eck's Introduction to Programming Using Java. No prose, examples, or exercises were copied; the source guides curriculum order only.
title: Generics
summary: Tell Java what type a collection is meant to hold.
refresher: Generics are the type names inside angle brackets, such as `ArrayList<String>`. They tell Java what kind of values belong in a collection. That lets Java catch the wrong type before the program runs.

blocks.body: Generics are the type labels inside angle brackets. In `ArrayList<String>`, the list is meant to hold Strings. That promise helps Java protect you from putting the wrong kind of value into the list.

blocks.caption: This list accepts String values. Getting an item returns a String, so no cast is needed.
blocks.body: Without generics, Java would not know what comes back out of a collection. With generics, `names.get(0)` is already known to be a String, so you do not have to guess or cast it by hand.
Without a type argument, an older raw list treats its items as Object. That loses useful checks and can move a type mistake from compilation to runtime.
blocks.caption: The list is for Strings. Java rejects a number before the program runs.
The generic list rejects the wrong item as soon as the code is compiled. The raw list accepts it and may fail during a later cast.
blocks.body: Angle brackets can look noisy at first. Read them as a label on the box: this box is for this type of thing.

blocks.title: Use reference types in angle brackets
blocks.body: Write `Integer` instead of `int` and `Double` instead of `double`. Java automatically wraps and unwraps these values in most collection code.

blocks.prompt: Which declaration creates a list that accepts Integer values?
blocks.why: The element type belongs inside angle brackets after List. Collections use the wrapper type Integer rather than the primitive type int.

blocks.body: Your own class can use a type parameter too. A Box<T> stores one value whose type is chosen when the object is declared. The same class can then make a Box<String> or Box<Student>.

choices: A type chosen when Box is used
The number of items in the box
blocks.why: T is a placeholder for a reference type. Declaring Box<String> replaces T with String for that use of the class.

blocks.title: Generics checklist
items: Put the element type in angle brackets.
Use ArrayList<String> for a list of Strings.
Let Java catch the wrong type early.
Avoid raw collections in new code.
Read generics as labels on containers.
